qa_pairs.question: What are the advantages of having multiple processes perform parallel I/O to a single file?
qa_pairs.answer: The advantages of having multiple processes perform parallel I/O to a single file are that there is only one file (which is good for visualization, data management, and storage), data can be stored canonically, and avoiding post-processing will scale if done correctly.

qa_pairs.question: What are the disadvantages of having multiple processes perform parallel I/O to a single file?
qa_pairs.answer: The disadvantages are that uncoordinated I/O will swamp the filesystem (due to file locks!) and it requires more design and thought.

qa_pairs.question: Why should parallel I/O be collective?
qa_pairs.answer: Parallel I/O should be collective because when coordinated writing, like collective I/O, is called, parallel middleware knows which processes and disks are involved, allowing it to find optimized operations in lower software layers for better efficiency.

qa_pairs.question: How does collective I/O improve efficiency in parallel I/O operations?
qa_pairs.answer: Collective I/O improves efficiency because when it's called, the parallel middleware identifies the processes and disks involved, then finds optimized operations in lower software layers to enhance overall performance.

qa_pairs.question: What role does parallel middleware play when collective I/O is used?
qa_pairs.answer: When collective I/O is used, parallel middleware determines which processes and disks will be involved in the writing operations. It then identifies and implements optimized operations in lower software layers to achieve better efficiency.
